Lexical tones take on 4 forms

# high
# low
# rising
# sinking
The first two can be present on the final syllable of a word and the second two the contour tones can only be present in non-final syllables.

Due to the etymology of words and how the tones originated it is impossible for there to be a sinking tone on the final syllable of a word.  because such homes only originated through reduction of complex consonant clusters that only occur in the middle of words clusters can occur at the beginning of words but at that point they are simply reduced into a different consonant and not into clusters into a sinking to however a rising tone is capable of occurring in a final syllable historically


A rising tone occurred in the word act (now á?ā)  because a rising tone comes from a cluster of at least two close and no other sound which is a relatively rare combination in English but it is totally possible to occur at the end of words

In the event that a rising tone is the last tone of a word the vowel is split  with a glottal stop  search that there is a rising tone on the vowel followed by a lot stop followed by the same vowel with a high tone.  This  is why act and actor are the same syllable count (á?ā & átaā)


There are not very many words like this but this has historically been a very productive way of creating effectively distinct morphemes


It has occurred with other tone changes because of the fact that many words lose their apparent relatedness after the tones and this is somewhat preserved in the writing but not entirely



bulk = baa

bulky = baakī


lexical tones and grammatical tones are both things that have the same phonological origin,  and as a result the grammatical tones actually emerged from the lexical tones of suffixes however because of them being grammatical it meant that they were treated slightly differently there was a higher emphasis placed on ensuring that the grammatical tones were always properly heard and that resulted in the development of the two complex contour tones of dipping tone and rising tone.  Originally both of those were treated as  two syllables with a sinking and then a rising syllable however they got shorter overtime.

== Minimal pair/quartet ==
{| class="wikitable"
|+
!
!
!
!
|-
|Atra
|
|
|
|-
|Astra
|
|
|
|-
|Akra
|
|
|
|-
|Ara
|
|
|
|}

== See Also ==

* [[Grammatical tones|Grammatical Tones]]

[[Category:Illish]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
